data.title: HSBC (LSE:HSBA) Stock Still Looks Undervalued Despite Its 418% Run
data.description:
HSBC Holdings has delivered a very large 418.4% return over the past five years, yet its valuation signals are split, with an intrinsic value estimate based on the Excess Returns model suggesting meaningful upside while earnings-based multiples lean expensive and the broader checks are cautious.

  • Over five years, shareholders have seen a 418.4% return, which puts extra focus on whether the current share price still leaves room for further gains.
  • HSBC's role in shaping expectations for US earnings growth across sectors such as energy, technology and industrials can support optimistic profit assumptions, but any setback to those earnings expectations may weigh quickly on what investors are willing to pay for the stock.
  • The stock only passes 2 of 6 valuation checks, which implies HSBC Holdings does not screen as a clear bargain on the broader measures even though the Excess Returns model suggests it may be undervalued by 31.6%.

The issue now is whether HSBC Holdings' recent share price strength already reflects this intrinsic value estimate, or if the current level still offers a reasonable margin of safety.

HSBC Holdings delivered 66.6% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Banks industry.

Advertisement

Does HSBC Holdings Look Undervalued on Excess Returns?

The Excess Returns model looks at how efficiently HSBC Holdings turns its equity base into profit after covering its cost of capital. On this view, the stock carries an intrinsic value estimate of about £22.31 per share, compared with the current market price. This implies a 31.6% discount.

For HSBC Holdings, the inputs are unusually rich. Book value sits at £10.17 per share and is projected to move toward a stable £11.68. Stable EPS of £1.87 per share, drawn from 15 analyst estimates, supports an average Return on Equity of 16.01%. After charging a £0.97 per share cost of equity, the model still leaves an excess return of £0.90 per share, which is what drives the higher fair value. Because HSBC now sees the strongest US Q2 earnings growth outlook since the immediate post pandemic period, the current gap between price and this return based valuation suggests the market may not be fully pricing in those profitability expectations.

On the Excess Returns view, HSBC Holdings stock currently screens as undervalued relative to the earnings power implied by its equity base.

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests HSBC Holdings is undervalued by 31.6%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 9 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for HSBC Holdings.

Is HSBC Holdings Getting Expensive on Earnings?

For a bank like HSBC Holdings, the P/E ratio is a useful way to see what investors are paying for each unit of current earnings.

HSBC Holdings currently trades on a P/E of about 16.6x, compared with an average of 11.6x for the wider Banks industry and a peer group average of around 11.9x. The Fair Ratio, which reflects what investors might typically pay for a bank with HSBC's profile, sits lower at roughly 11.2x. That gap suggests the market is assigning a premium to HSBC's earnings relative to both sector norms and what the tailored model implies.

Based on this framework, HSBC Holdings stock currently appears overvalued on its earnings multiple, without taking into account any potential changes in future profit expectations.

On the P/E measure, HSBC Holdings currently looks priced on the expensive side relative to its earnings.

The HSBC Holdings Narrative: What Would Justify Today's Price?

Simply Wall St Narratives for HSBC Holdings pick up where the valuation puzzle leaves off by spelling out the specific assumptions about HSBC Holdings' future growth, margins and earnings that would make the current stock price look materially higher or lower. Instead of giving just a single figure from a ratio or model, they set out the future that figure relies on, so you can monitor whether those conditions actually play out over time on the Community page.

The community is split on HSBC Holdings, with one camp leaning into the Asia and Wealth build out while another worries that execution and regional risks already look fully reflected.

Bull case: 12% undervalued

"Rapid expansion of Wealth and new customer flows in Asia, including US$29b of net new invested assets with more than half from Asia and over 900,000 new to bank customers year to date, shows HSBC is plugged into long term growth in cross border capital and rising affluent wealth..."

Bear case: 6% overvalued

"Continued exposure and strategic reliance on growth from Asia, particularly Hong Kong and mainland China, leaves HSBC vulnerable to region-specific macroeconomic slowdowns, heightened regulatory crackdowns, or geopolitical tensions, potentially dampening top-line revenue growth and eroding net margins..."

Read the full Bear Case to see why HSBC Holdings could be overvalued

The Bottom Line

For HSBC Holdings, the Excess Returns intrinsic value estimate points to meaningful upside, while the P/E comparison flags the stock as overvalued versus typical bank peers. That split, along with a low value score, suggests the optimism in the share price leans heavily on earnings holding up strongly against its capital base. Much of the gap comes down to how you weigh cash flow durability against already full market expectations after a very large multi year move. The key question from here is whether HSBC’s Asia and wealth expansion can sustain the profitability that the current rating appears to assume, rather than leaving the stock as a value trap.

data.title: AppLovin Stock Leads 3 High Growth Insider Owned Tech Picks
data.description:
With central banks keeping rates tight, bond yields elevated and energy prices adding extra inflation risk, investors are paying closer attention to companies that can grow from their own steam. That is where fast growing stocks with high insider ownership come in. When management and founders hold a meaningful stake, their interests are closely aligned with yours, which can be appealing when policy paths differ across regions and economic signals are mixed. This article highlights 3 stocks from the Fast Growing Stocks With High Insider Ownership screener that stand out for further research.

Advertisement

Klarna Group (KLAR)

Overview: Klarna Group is a London based digital bank and flexible payments provider that lets shoppers pay in full, pay later or spread purchases over several months across markets including the US, Germany, Sweden and the UK. Alongside these payment options, Klarna also runs a shopping app with product search, price comparison, advertising services and digital banking features such as savings and deposit accounts.

Operations: Klarna generates all of its US$3.8b in revenue from data processing activities, with key markets including the United States (US$1.4b), Germany (US$888m), the United Kingdom (US$468m) and other countries (US$1.1b).

Market Cap: US$7.2b

Investors looking at Klarna Group are getting a fast growing payments platform that is edging toward profitability, with earnings forecast to rise 52.63% a year and revenue expected to grow 15.5% a year, but also a business that still reports losses and has no customer deposits, so it relies on external funding. Recent securitization deals, the pursuit of a US banking license and an Apple partnership show how Klarna is trying to scale its lending and merchant reach while keeping capital use efficient. At the same time, a large potential antitrust payout from Google could reshape its balance sheet. The key consideration for shareholders is how these different factors will interact over the next few years.

Klarna Group’s rapid shift toward profitability, new funding channels and potential antitrust payout make the story feel incomplete. See how the Klarna Group financial health report could change the risk reward picture in ways the headline numbers miss.

Hinge Health (HNGE)

Overview: Hinge Health is a San Francisco based digital health company that uses software, AI powered motion tracking and a nerve stimulation wearable to deliver musculoskeletal care for issues like chronic pain, injuries and post surgery rehab. It mainly works with self insured employers and health plans, aiming to cut costs and speed up recovery by moving much of this care into a structured virtual program.

Operations: Hinge Health generates all of its US$646.3m in revenue from healthcare software.

Market Cap: US$6.5b

Hinge Health gives you exposure to technology driven healthcare, with musculoskeletal care at its core and AI being integrated into both clinical workflows and product development. The company focuses on employer demand for measurable cost savings and new programs like HingeSelect and migraine care. At the same time, the stock trades at a high multiple of sales and relies on external funding, while execution risk around AI, regulation and any expansion beyond core MSK care remains significant. The key consideration is how these factors may affect long term shareholders and whether current expectations allow for setbacks.

Hinge Health’s push to use AI and virtual care to cut employer healthcare costs is gaining attention, but expectations are already high. See how the analyst forecasts for Hinge Health reframes the upside and what might be missing.

AppLovin (APP)

Overview: AppLovin is a Palo Alto based company that runs an AI powered advertising platform, helping app and e commerce businesses target users, measure performance and monetize their content across mobile and connected TV. Through tools like its Axon Ads Manager, MAX bidding tech, Adjust analytics and Wurl streaming platform, AppLovin connects advertisers with mobile app publishers and developers worldwide.

Operations: AppLovin generates US$6.2b in revenue from its Advertising segment, split between the United States at US$3.1b and the rest of the world at US$3.0b.

Market Cap: US$144.0b

AppLovin combines AI driven advertising, high reported margins and a large buyback program, which is why many investors are watching the stock despite a premium P/E and recent share price pullback. The company is focusing on its Axon AI platform to help improve targeting and returns for advertisers, while expanding beyond mobile gaming into e commerce and other categories. This may help diversify revenue. At the same time, reliance on mobile platforms, exposure to changing privacy rules and significant competition from players such as Meta and Google all keep risk elevated. Investors are weighing whether AppLovin’s growth, cash generation and capital returns are sufficient to offset those pressures and the valuation gap to analyst targets.

data.title: 3 Indian Penny Stocks With Positive Earnings That Look Cheap
data.description:
Penny stocks often get attention for the wrong reasons, but a carefully filtered group can offer targeted exposure when global markets are dominated by rising bond yields, energy driven inflation risks and shifting rate expectations from the ECB, Fed and other central banks. The Financially Fit Penny Stocks screener focuses on companies trading below 5 that also show signs of solid financial health, helping you focus on balance sheet strength rather than hype. In this article, you will see 3 stocks from this screener, selected for investors who want higher risk opportunities with a more disciplined starting point.

Advertisement

Swasti Vinayaka Synthetics (BSE:510245)

Overview: Swasti Vinayaka Synthetics is a Mumbai based textile company that manufactures and sells shirting, suiting and ethnic apparel fabrics across cotton, linen, lycra, polyester, viscose and blended materials, and also designs bridal and formal wear under its Ivy League brand for both retail buyers and institutional customers such as corporates and government bodies.

Operations: The company generates all of its ₹320.3m in revenue from manufacturing a wide range of shirting fabrics in India.

Market Cap: ₹331.2m

Swasti Vinayaka Synthetics catches the eye because earnings growth of 25.9% over the past year has come with improving net profit margins at 9.5%, even as full year revenue of ₹338.7m and sales of ₹320.3m softened. The stock is currently described as trading 86% below an internal fair value estimate, with a P/E of 10.9x that is lower than both sector and peer averages. This profile may appeal to value focused investors who still care about earnings quality. At the same time, all liabilities come from higher risk external borrowing and revenue is still relatively small, so funding and scale are important watchpoints. An experienced management team and board add context to this mix of improving profitability and balance sheet risk.

Swasti Vinayaka Synthetics looks like a valuation story that many investors may be overlooking, with a P/E of 10.9x and claims of trading 86% below fair value potentially masking a key twist in the DCF valuation analysis for Swasti Vinayaka Synthetics

Swasti Vinayaka Art and Heritage (BSE:512257)

Overview: Swasti Vinayaka Art and Heritage is a Mumbai based company that creates and sells carvings in precious and semi precious stones, paintings and jewelry, while also being involved in real estate activities in India.

Market Cap: ₹324.0m

Swasti Vinayaka Art and Heritage is likely to stand out on this penny stock list because its small scale sits alongside a very low P/E of 5.6x. It has a long run of earnings growth around 14.1% a year and a sharp improvement in profitability, with net profit margins at 18.3%. Recent full year figures show revenue of ₹330.7m and net income of ₹58.08m, which supports the story of stronger earnings, but also highlights that the business is still relatively modest in size and heavily reliant on higher risk external funding. For investors who want potential mispricing but do not want to ignore funding and governance quality, the balance of strong recent earnings, an experienced board and elevated balance sheet risk makes this one to study more closely.

Swasti Vinayaka Art and Heritage pairs a low P/E with stronger profitability, which raises an obvious question: is the stock just cheap, or is the market flagging something deeper that shows up in the 2 key rewards and 1 important warning sign?

Shangar Décor (BSE:540259)

Overview: Shangar Décor is an Ahmedabad based events company that handles decoration and event management for pre wedding functions, theme weddings, corporate gatherings, religious occasions and property and lighting decor, and also offers catering, providing an end to end service for clients across India.

Operations: Shangar Décor generates its entire ₹232.6m in revenue from event management services in India.

Market Cap: ₹117.5m

Shangar Décor may interest you if you are looking for a small events stock where the story is shifting toward improving profitability and an apparently cheap valuation. Earnings growth of 82.3% over the past year, higher net margins at 6.3% and a Q4 swing from loss to profit hint at a business that is starting to turn its scale into more consistent earnings, even if revenue is still modest at ₹232.6m. A low P/E relative to the Indian Consumer Services sector and a price far below an internal fair value estimate sit beside low 2.5% ROE, a history of share price volatility and reliance on external borrowing. The real question is whether this mix of momentum and balance sheet risk still stacks up for you as an investor.

data.title: NuScale Power (SMR) Stock Looks About Right Despite The ENTRA1 Probe
data.description:
NuScale Power stock has fallen sharply over the past year, yet current valuation checks suggest it does not clearly stand out as a bargain. This puts extra focus on whether recent news and risk perceptions are fully reflected in the price.

  • Over the last 12 months, NuScale Power has declined 81.9%, which signals a major reset in market expectations around its small modular reactor story.
  • NuScale Power’s position as a U.S. developer of small modular reactors for data center and AI energy demand can support long term potential, but recent shareholder litigation over its ENTRA1 partnership and investigations into its rollout plans underline execution and credibility risks that investors now have to factor into valuation.
  • NuScale Power scores 1 out of 6 on the broader valuation checks, which points to a stock that currently leans expensive rather than obviously cheap on these measures.

The issue now is whether NuScale Power’s current share price already reflects these risks, or if the market is still paying too much for its future potential.

Find out why NuScale Power's -81.9% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Is NuScale Power Fairly Priced on Book Value?

P/B works reasonably well for NuScale Power because the company is still focused on building out its asset and project base rather than generating consistent profits.

NuScale Power trades at a P/B of about 2.6x, which is close to the Electrical industry average of around 2.5x and far below the peer group average of roughly 29.6x. That suggests the market is valuing each dollar of NuScale Power’s equity similarly to the broader industry, despite its small modular reactor focus and early stage profile.

Because the ENTRA1 probe keeps execution and credibility risks in the spotlight, this alignment with the industry multiple implies investors are already applying some caution instead of awarding a clear premium. On this P/B view, NuScale Power does not screen as a glaring outlier in either direction.

Overall, NuScale Power currently trades at a level that is broadly consistent with its book value, so on a P/B basis the stock does not appear significantly misaligned with that metric.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) Unit CORT Is Buying Dwellworks Living
data.description:
• CORT Business Services, a Berkshire Hathaway subsidiary, has agreed to acquire Dwellworks Living.
  • The deal expands CORT's reach in global mobility and temporary living solutions.
  • The move adds to Berkshire Hathaway's non financial business services portfolio.

Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A), trading at around $732,750.01 per share, continues to lean on its diverse collection of operating companies to shape its long term profile. The stock has risen 37.8% over the past 3 years and 74.9% over the past 5 years, which highlights the role of its broad business mix beyond headline discussions about its investment portfolio.

This latest acquisition by CORT Business Services points to further build out in business services linked to housing and global workforce mobility. Investors tracking Berkshire Hathaway may want to watch how this deal fits into the company’s approach to recurring service revenues and its exposure to corporate relocation and temporary housing demand over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Berkshire Hathaway by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Berkshire Hathaway.

📰 Beyond the headline: 1 risk and 2 things going right for Berkshire Hathaway that every investor should see.

For Berkshire Hathaway, CORT’s agreement to acquire Dwellworks Living looks like a continuation of the push into fee-based business services that sit alongside its core insurance and industrial holdings. Dwellworks Living adds a global temporary housing platform that serves relocating employees and corporate clients, which can complement CORT’s existing furniture rental and relocation services. That combination gives Berkshire Hathaway deeper exposure to move-in ready housing solutions tied to corporate mobility programs, rather than relying only on traditional residential demand. The transaction also lengthens Berkshire Hathaway’s chain of touchpoints around housing, from owned real estate activities in other subsidiaries through to short term stays and relocation support, which may matter for investors who follow how its non financial operations contribute to overall stability.

Advertisement

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Greater exposure to global mobility and corporate relocation links part of Berkshire Hathaway’s earnings to trends in hiring, business travel, and project based work, which can cool if companies slow expansion plans.
  • ⚠️ Integrating Dwellworks Living into CORT introduces execution risk around systems, culture, and service levels, particularly if large multinational clients expect seamless global coverage from day one.
  • 🎁 Adding Dwellworks Living broadens Berkshire Hathaway’s non financial services footprint into global temporary housing, giving the group another potential source of relatively steady, contract based revenue.
  • 🎁 CORT’s larger presence in global mobility services could deepen relationships with corporate customers and relocation management firms, potentially creating cross selling opportunities across Berkshire Hathaway’s wider business services portfolio.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth watching how CORT reports on occupancy, average contract length, and client retention at Dwellworks Living once the deal closes, as these will be key indicators of how the acquisition is bedding in. Investors in Berkshire Hathaway can also track whether management highlights this business more frequently in annual reports or shareholder communications, especially if recurring service revenues from housing and mobility start to feature more prominently in the non insurance segment mix. Any commentary on further acquisitions in related areas, or on new partnerships with large employers and relocation specialists, will help clarify how important this expanded global mobility platform becomes within the wider group over time.

Operations: Larsen & Toubro generates most of its revenue from Infrastructure Projects at ₹1,354.2b, followed by IT & Technology Services at ₹545.7b, Energy Projects at ₹549.0b, Financial Services at ₹178.5b and Hi Tech Manufacturing at ₹144.9b, with additional contributions from Development Projects and Others.

data.title: Best ASX Stocks For Real Estate Exposure Without Buying Property
data.description:
Owning your own slice of Australia has long been considered the “Great Australian dream”.

But, not only is residential real estate increasingly facing an affordability issue, but it’s also only a small slice of the potential opportunities that are out there for investors looking to profit from property.

Property is not just housing. It is office buildings, hospitals, and shopping centres, to name but a few. There are also supporting industries, most notably mortgage finance and other forms of credit, as well as building materials, that, while not being traditional property businesses, are driven by the same factors.

And at any given time, companies in these sectors can offer greater potential returns and diversified risk compared to focusing only on residential real estate.

Below are three of possibly the best ASX stocks that could present good options for people looking for property exposure, but without actually buying property.

Advertisement

Qualitas (ASX:QAL)

Qualitas promises “unrivalled expertise in real estate investment management” and its ability to prove that is why it is one of the best ASX stocks for investors looking for exposure to property.

Qualitas focuses on investing in a diverse range of opportunities in the property sector across geographies and asset classes, with a strong focus on debt and private credit for property projects.

As an investment manager, Qualitas doesn’t necessarily own property. Instead, it creates funds that invest in either property directly or the financing vehicles that allow properties to be built or managed. Its main source of revenue comes from management fees from its funds rather than rent from tenants. This also means that its core risks come from borrowers not paying their financing rather than tenants not paying rent, which is different to traditional real estate ownership.

Qualitas allows investors to invest in a property-exposed asset class that is not readily available to the average person.

Want to see what the Simply Wall Street Community thinks about Qualitas? Here is what the most followed narrative has to say.

Helia (ASX:HLI)

Helia is one of Australia’s largest providers of lenders' mortgage insurance and one of the less obvious candidates for the title of best ASX stock for real estate-focused investors.

Lenders’ mortgage insurance protects lenders who provide finance to people with a small deposit. It protects the lender from the risk that the borrower is unable to make repayments and the lender is unable to recover the loan amount from the sale of the property.

This allows lenders to significantly open up the number of people able to take out a home loan, with some able to do so with a deposit of as little as 5% of the purchase price. Without lenders’ mortgage insurance, a large number of Australians would not be able to attempt to live the Australian dream. However, it is worth noting that because the product Helia offers is there to protect lenders from borrowers defaulting, it is also highly at risk of an economic downturn or property crash.

Helia is yet another company that has managed to find a niche that, while not directly owning property, is being driven by the success of the Australian property market.

WHSP Holdings (ASX:SOL)

Finally, we have one of the oldest and most respected companies on the ASX, WHSP Holdings.

With over a hundred years of history, WHSP, which is now the amalgamation of Washington H. Soul Pattinson and Brickworks, after they merged in 2025, is often cited as Australia’s answer to Berkshire Hathaway. It also boasts around 123 years of never cutting its dividend.

Through its Brickworks business, which is one of Australia’s largest building products manufacturers. WHSP also operates a series of investment funds relating to real estate and private credit, amongst others, offering investors a high-quality and diversified exposure to real estate (as well as other industries), being managed by some of the most well-respected names in the industry.

With a long history of market-beating returns, quality management and strong assets, WHSP Holdings could be one of the best ASX stocks for investors wanting exposure to real estate.

More than a home

data.title: Be Sure To Check Out KT Corporation (KRX:030200) Before It Goes Ex-Dividend
data.description:
Regular readers will know that we love our dividends at Simply Wall St, which is why it's exciting to see KT Corporation (KRX:030200) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next 4 days. Typically, the ex-dividend date is two business days before the record date, which is the date on which a company determines the shareholders eligible to receive a dividend. It is important to be aware of the ex-dividend date because any trade on the stock needs to have been settled on or before the record date. Thus, you can purchase KT's shares before the 28th of July in order to receive the dividend, which the company will pay on the 13th of August.

The company's next dividend payment will be ₩600.00 per share, and in the last 12 months, the company paid a total of ₩2,400 per share. Based on the last year's worth of payments, KT has a trailing yield of 4.6% on the current stock price of ₩52700.00. Dividends are an important source of income to many shareholders, but the health of the business is crucial to maintaining those dividends. So we need to investigate whether KT can afford its dividend, and if the dividend could grow.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Dividends are typically paid from company earnings. If a company pays more in dividends than it earned in profit, then the dividend could be unsustainable. That's why it's good to see KT paying out a modest 38% of its earnings. Yet cash flows are even more important than profits for assessing a dividend, so we need to see if the company generated enough cash to pay its distribution. It distributed 33% of its free cash flow as dividends, a comfortable payout level for most companies.

It's encouraging to see that the dividend is covered by both profit and cash flow. This generally suggests the dividend is sustainable, as long as earnings don't drop precipitously.

View our latest analysis for KT

Click here to see the company's payout ratio, plus analyst estimates of its future dividends.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Stocks in companies that generate sustainable earnings growth often make the best dividend prospects, as it is easier to lift the dividend when earnings are rising. If business enters a downturn and the dividend is cut, the company could see its value fall precipitously. Fortunately for readers, KT's earnings per share have been growing at 17% a year for the past five years. Earnings per share are growing rapidly and the company is keeping more than half of its earnings within the business; an attractive combination which could suggest the company is focused on reinvesting to grow earnings further. This will make it easier to fund future growth efforts and we think this is an attractive combination - plus the dividend can always be increased later.

Another key way to measure a company's dividend prospects is by measuring its historical rate of dividend growth. In the last 10 years, KT has lifted its dividend by approximately 17% a year on average. It's great to see earnings per share growing rapidly over several years, and dividends per share growing right along with it.

To Sum It Up

Has KT got what it takes to maintain its dividend payments? KT has been growing earnings at a rapid rate, and has a conservatively low payout ratio, implying that it is reinvesting heavily in its business; a sterling combination. It's a promising combination that should mark this company worthy of closer attention.
